label: Port-au-Prince City Center
sub: Haiti's capital and most conflict-affected urban zone. Gangs control approximately 90% of the city. The National Palace and key government buildings are in a narrow government-held corridor.

label: Cité Soleil
sub: Densely populated waterfront commune entirely controlled by rival gang factions. Site of the July 2022 G9 vs. G-Pèp massacre (207 dead) and December 2024 Wharf Jérémie massacre (207 dead). Home to over 300,000 people in extreme poverty.

label: Wharf Jérémie (Dec 2024 Massacre)
sub: Site of the December 6–11, 2024 massacre in which gang leader Micanor Altes killed 207 confirmed civilians, including elderly Vodou practitioners. Bodies were burned and thrown into the sea. UN designated it an atrocity.

label: Varreux Fuel Terminal
sub: Haiti's main petroleum import terminal, supplying ~70% of national fuel. Blockaded by G9 gangs in October 2021 and again in September 2022, triggering humanitarian crises in hospitals and water systems. Recaptured by MSS in 2024.

label: Toussaint Louverture Intl Airport
sub: Haiti's main international airport, attacked by gangs on March 4, 2024, forcing closure to commercial flights. Airport remained closed for months, isolating Haiti from international aid and commerce. MSS forces secured the perimeter in mid-2024.

label: National Palace
sub: Haiti's presidential palace and seat of government. Damaged in the 2010 earthquake. Site where the Transitional Presidential Council was sworn in on April 25, 2024. Lies in a narrow government-controlled corridor within Port-au-Prince.

label: Hôpital de l'Université d'État d'Haïti (HUEH)
sub: Haiti's main public hospital, repeatedly threatened by gang violence. Faced critical fuel shortages during 2021 and 2022 terminal blockades. MSS forces helped secure its perimeter in 2024 but the facility remains severely understaffed and underfunded.

label: Bel Air (Gang Zone)
sub: Urban neighborhood in northern Port-au-Prince controlled by G9-affiliated gangs. Key node in the gang corridor linking Cité Soleil to the city center. Site of the 2018 La Saline massacre and ongoing gang operations.

label: La Saline
sub: Waterfront slum and gang stronghold. Site of the 2018 La Saline massacre (71 killed) under G9 affiliated groups. Remains under gang control and serves as a gateway to port facilities and Varreux terminal.

label: Martissant
sub: Chokepoint neighborhood on Route Nationale 2, the only road connecting Port-au-Prince to Haiti's southern peninsula. Gang control effectively severed the south from the capital since 2021. MSF was forced to suspend its emergency hospital operations here repeatedly.

label: Delmas
sub: Sprawling middle-class district increasingly affected by gang expansion from the north and west. Multiple police stations have been attacked. Significant IDP population sheltered in local schools and churches since 2022.

label: Pétion-Ville
sub: Affluent hillside suburb and diplomatic hub where the Montana Accord was formed in August 2021. Home to international NGOs, embassies, and upper-middle-class residents. Increasingly targeted by kidnappers despite historically lower violence levels.

label: Tabarre IDP Sites
sub: Area northeast of Port-au-Prince hosting multiple IDP displacement sites since 2022. Thousands of families displaced from Cité Soleil, Martissant, and other gang-controlled areas. Sites face severe overcrowding, lack of sanitation, and cholera risk.

label: Croix-des-Bouquets National Penitentiary
sub: Site of a coordinated gang prison break on March 2, 2024, which freed approximately 2,000 inmates. The warden was killed in the assault. The breakout formed part of Viv Ansanm's broader February–March 2024 offensive that precipitated PM Henry's departure.

label: Port-au-Prince Civil Prison
sub: Haiti's main civil detention facility, stormed by gang forces on March 3, 2024, freeing approximately 2,700 inmates. The coordinated prison breaks—alongside the airport attack—marked the peak of Viv Ansanm's offensive that ousted PM Henry.

label: MSS / GSF Forward Operating Base
sub: Primary forward operating base for the Kenya-led Multinational Security Support Mission (MSS), now transitioning to the Gang Suppression Force (GSF). Houses Kenyan police contingent and coordinates joint operations with the Haitian National Police.

label: Kenyan MSS Operational Hub
sub: Coordination hub for Kenyan National Police Service officers deployed under the MSS mandate. Houses logistics, communications, and command infrastructure. First Kenyan officer killed in combat, Samuel Tompei Kaetuai, was based at this facility before his death on February 24, 2025.

label: UN BINUH Office
sub: Headquarters of the United Nations Integrated Office in Haiti (BINUH), mandated to support political stability and human rights monitoring. Repeatedly reported on gang violence, IDP conditions, and government failures. Mandate extended through 2026.

label: Cap-Haïtien
sub: Haiti's second-largest city in the north. Historically more stable than Port-au-Prince but increasingly affected by gang expansion in 2024–2025. Cap-Haïtien airport experienced closure due to gunfire incidents and serves as an alternate entry point for MSS supply lines.

label: Les Cayes
sub: Southern Haiti's main city, devastated by the August 14, 2021, 7.2 magnitude earthquake that killed 2,200+ people. MSF and international NGOs established field hospitals here. The Martissant gang blockade isolated this region from Port-au-Prince aid for extended periods.

label: Jacmel
sub: Southern coastal city and cultural hub. Affected by the 2021 earthquake and the Martissant corridor blockade that periodically severed road access from the capital. Jacmel's port serves as an alternative supply route for the region's 600,000+ residents.

label: Jérémie
sub: Capital of the Grand'Anse department on Haiti's southern peninsula. Heavily damaged by the 2021 earthquake and isolated by the Martissant blockade. Receives humanitarian supplies primarily by sea and air when road access is cut.

label: Gonaïves
sub: Capital of the Artibonite Department and Haiti's third-largest city. Located on Route Nationale 1, the main artery to the north. Gang expansion into the Artibonite Valley in 2024–2025 threatened access to this historically significant 'City of Independence.'

label: Pont-Sondé (Oct 2024 Massacre)
sub: Town in the Artibonite Department attacked by the Gran Grif gang on October 3, 2024. At least 115 civilians killed, including infants and elderly, when gunmen arrived by canoe. Over 6,270 residents fled. The attack demonstrated the spread of gang violence beyond Port-au-Prince.

label: Saint-Marc
sub: Port city in Artibonite, gateway to the Artibonite Valley rice-growing region. Gang expansion in 2024–2025 threatened the area's agricultural economy. A key point on Route Nationale 1 where gangs have established checkpoints and extortion operations.

label: Hinche
sub: Capital of the Centre Department, an historically quiet agricultural region. Gang expansion from Port-au-Prince reached Centre in 2024–2025, with BINUH reports noting violence flaring in communities along the Route du Centre.

label: Port-de-Paix
sub: Capital of the Northwest Department. Gang activities in the northwest escalated in 2025 as Viv Ansanm extended influence along routes toward the Dominican border. The region had limited MSS presence.

label: Miragoâne
sub: Southern port city in Nippes Department. Key entry point for humanitarian supplies into the south after the Martissant blockade closed Route Nationale 2. MSF operated medical facilities in the region serving earthquake-affected and displaced populations.

label: Arcahaie
sub: Coastal town on Route Nationale 1 north of Port-au-Prince. Gang-controlled checkpoints in the Arcahaie area effectively tax or block cargo trucks heading to the capital, contributing to supply shortages. Agricultural produce from northern Haiti must pass through this corridor.

label: Canaan / Jerusalem IDP Zone
sub: Massive informal settlement northeast of Port-au-Prince, home to hundreds of thousands who fled the 2010 earthquake and never returned. Serves as a receiving area for new IDP waves from gang violence in 2021–2025. Limited services, high disease burden, and cholera outbreaks.

label: Champ de Mars IDP Sites
sub: Public spaces around the National Palace converted to IDP shelters. Thousands of families displaced from gang-controlled zones live in makeshift camps with minimal sanitation. UN reports document cholera cases and sexual violence targeting displaced women and girls.

label: MSF Tabarre Emergency Hospital
sub: MSF trauma hospital in Tabarre, one of the few facilities able to treat gunshot and machete wounds during Port-au-Prince gang violence. Faced critical fuel shortages during 2022 Varreux blockades. Performs hundreds of complex surgical procedures monthly.

label: MSF Martissant Emergency Centre
sub: MSF emergency and trauma center in Martissant, the gang-controlled chokepoint on Route Nationale 2. The facility was forced to suspend operations multiple times between 2021–2025 due to direct armed attacks, sniper fire, and gang incursions. Thousands of patients lost access to emergency care.

label: OCHA Haiti Coordination Hub
sub: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs hub managing Haiti's humanitarian response. Coordinates between 200+ NGOs and UN agencies. Issues regular situation reports on IDP numbers, food insecurity, cholera, and humanitarian access constraints.

label: Carrefour
sub: Large suburban commune west of Port-au-Prince along the coast. Heavy gang presence with multiple armed groups competing for control. Carrefour lies on the western approach to Route Nationale 2 and is a key node in the gang stranglehold on southern Haiti.

label: Léogâne
sub: Coastal town that suffered extensive damage in the 2010 earthquake. Since 2021, gang activity on Route Nationale 2 has periodically cut off humanitarian access. The town serves as a reference point for WHO cholera monitoring in the southern corridor.

label: Pelerin 5 (Moïse Assassination Site)
sub: Hillside neighborhood of Pétion-Ville where President Jovenel Moïse was assassinated at his private residence on July 7, 2021. The attack was carried out by a team of 28 mercenaries, mostly Colombian nationals. The site became a focal point for investigations and political controversy.

label: Artibonite Valley (Agricultural Zone)
sub: Haiti's most important rice-growing region, feeding millions. Gang expansion by Gran Grif and other groups in 2023–2025 disrupted planting seasons, extorted farmers, and forced displacement. Loss of Artibonite production dramatically worsened food insecurity.

label: Jean-Denis (Mar 2026 Massacre)
sub: Site of the March 30, 2026 Gran Grif gang massacre near Pont-Sondé. Pre-dawn attack killed at least 16 confirmed (up to 70 per RNDDH) and wounded ~30 civilians, displacing nearly 6,000 residents. Gran Grif retaliated against the community following an assault on its base in Savien by a rival group. Attack echoed the October 2024 Pont-Sondé massacre by the same gang.

label: Fort-Liberté
sub: Capital of the Northeast Department, near the Dominican border. As gangs expanded northward along Route Nationale 6 in 2025, Fort-Liberté became a point of concern for cross-border trafficking and gang movement toward the Dominican frontier.

label: Haitian National Police (PNH) HQ
sub: Headquarters of the Haitian National Police, which had an estimated active force of 15,000–17,000 officers in a country of 12 million. Heavily infiltrated by gang informants, under-equipped, and frequently outgunned. Multiple police stations were seized during the February–March 2024 gang offensive.

label: Kenyan Embassy / MSS Liaison
sub: Kenyan diplomatic mission in Port-au-Prince serving as a key administrative liaison for the MSS deployment. The embassy managed bilateral coordination between the Haitian Transitional Council and the Kenyan National Police Service command.

label: Artibonite Cholera Cluster
sub: The Artibonite region was one of the original epicenters of the UN-linked cholera outbreak that began in 2010. The 2022 resurgence also started here. Gangs control water infrastructure and block public health responses, allowing cholera to spread through displacement camps and unprotected water sources.

label: UN Support Office in Haiti (UNSOH)
sub: Headquarters of the newly established United Nations Support Office in Haiti (UNSOH), created to provide logistics, intelligence sharing, and operational support to the Gang Suppression Force (GSF). Daniela Kroslak appointed as UNSOH head in March 2026. Replaces prior MSS logistics coordination functions.

label: Marchand Dessalines (Gran Grif Advance, Apr 2026)
sub: Strategic Artibonite town targeted by Gran Grif and Kokorat San Ras gangs following the March 30, 2026 Jean-Denis massacre. Gang members filmed distributing cash to residents — a control tactic — as forces advanced ~19 km north of the massacre site. The offensive threatened Haiti's most important rice-growing region ahead of planting season.

label: Mirebalais (University Hospital — Closed)
sub: The University Hospital in Mirebalais, Centre Department, was shuttered due to gang violence expanding into the Centre Department in 2024–2025. The facility previously served 185,000 patients. Its closure — one of several hospital shutdowns across Haiti — contributed to an acute healthcare access crisis as gangs expanded beyond Port-au-Prince.
